title: iterator - What does the "yield" keyword do in Python? - Stack Overflow
body: 13133 What functionality does the yield keyword in Python provide? For example, I'm trying to understand this code 1 : def _get_child_candidates(self, distance, min_dist, max_dist): if self._leftchild and distance - max_dist < self._median: yield self._leftchild if self._rightchild and distance + max_dist >= self._median: yield self._rightchild And this is the caller: result, candidates = [], [self] while candidates: node = candidates.pop() distance = node._get_dist(obj) if distance <= max_dist and distance >= min_dist: result.extend(node._values) candidates.extend(node._get_child_candidates(distance, min_dist, max_dist)) return result What happens when the method _get_child_candidates is called? Is a list returned? A single element? Is it called again? When will subsequent calls stop? 1. This piece of code was written by Jochen Schulz (jrschulz), who made a great Python library for metric spaces. This is the link to the complete source: Module mspace . python iterator generator yield Share Improve this question Follow edited Mar 24, 2024 at 4:11 Mateen Ulhaq 28.1k 22 22 gold badges 122 122 silver badges 157 157 bronze badges asked Oct 23, 2008 at 22:21 Alex. S. 148k 21 21 gold badges 58 58 silver badges 62 62 bronze badges 5 7 Guys the line: if distance <= max_dist and distance >= min_dist: can be shortened to if min_dist <= distance <= max_dist: Walter – Walter 2024-04-22 10:49:31 +00:00 Commented Apr 22, 2024 at 10:49 9 Yield in Python used to create a generator function. Generator function behaves like an iterator, which can be used in loop to retrieve items one at a time. When a generator function is called, it returns a generator object without executing the function immediately. When next() is called on the generator object, the function executes until it reaches a yield statement, which returns the yielded value and pauses the function's execution, maintaining its state. When next() is called again, the function resumes execution right after the yield statement, until it reaches another yield or returns. hassan javaid – hassan javaid 2024-05-23 13:23:54 +00:00 Commented May 23, 2024 at 13:23 If I was the author of the documentation of the yield function, I would be ashamed that this question has 10k+ upvotes :D Came here clueless after reading the docs. Matthias Luh – Matthias Luh 2025-12-21 15:59:01 +00:00 Commented Dec 21, 2025 at 15:59 @MatthiasLuh If I was the author of the documentation of the yield function, I would be relieved to see that it took fifty different stack exchange answers to address this question, clearly demonstrating how challenging this is to explain in a way that everyone can understand at the same time. uhoh – uhoh 2025-12-22 01:15:57 +00:00 Commented Dec 22, 2025 at 1:15 @uhoh "yield is a keyword that is used like return, except the function will return a generator." by the user "Bite code" is the sentence that caused the aha moment for me. And if the documentation is not only written for people that already know what they're doing, examples are always helpful as well. Matthias Luh – Matthias Luh 2025-12-22 10:41:35 +00:00 Commented Dec 22, 2025 at 10:41 Add a comment |
